section.title: Remember His Wonderful Works
section.sectionRationale: A historical hymn in Book IV, Psalms 90 to 106, that rehearses the covenant faithfulness of God from Abraham through Jacob, Joseph, the Exodus, and the wilderness, into the gift of Canaan. Its opening verses are quoted almost verbatim in David's song at the bringing of the ark, 1 Chronicles 16:8-22, and it forms a deliberate pair with Psalm 106, which follows immediately: this psalm celebrates what God did, that psalm confesses how Israel responded.
section.thesis: The LORD who chose Abraham and swore an everlasting covenant to his offspring has kept every word of it, protecting a small wandering family, sending Joseph ahead into Egypt, humbling Pharaoh with wonders, and bringing His people out with joy into the land He promised, so that remembering His wonders is itself the fuel of praise.
section.frameNote: Eight movements: a call to praise and remember, verses 1 to 6; the everlasting covenant, verses 7 to 11; the patriarchs protected among the nations, verses 12 to 15; Joseph sent ahead into Egypt, verses 16 to 22; Israel multiplying in Egypt, verses 23 to 25; Moses, Aaron, and the plagues, verses 26 to 36; the exodus and wilderness provision, verses 37 to 41; and the land given so that Israel might keep His statutes, verses 42 to 45.

units.label: Give thanks to the LORD
units.note: a call to praise and remember

verses.text: Give thanks to the LORD, call upon His name; make known His deeds among the nations.
Sing to Him, sing praises to Him; tell of all His wonders.
Glory in His holy name; let the hearts of those who seek the LORD rejoice.
Seek out the LORD and His strength; seek His face always.
Remember the {{niflaot|wonders}} He has done, His marvels, and the judgments He has pronounced,
O offspring of His servant Abraham, O sons of Jacob, His chosen ones.
He is the LORD our God; His judgments carry throughout the earth.
He remembers His {{berit|covenant}} forever, the word He ordained for a thousand generations,
the covenant He made with Abraham, and the oath He swore to Isaac.
He confirmed it to Jacob as a decree, to Israel as an everlasting covenant:
"I will give you the land of Canaan as the portion of your inheritance."

units.label: He rebuked kings on their behalf
units.note: the patriarchs protected among the nations

verses.text: When they were few in number, few indeed, and strangers in the land,
they wandered from nation to nation, from one kingdom to another.
He let no man oppress them; He rebuked kings on their behalf:
"Do not touch My {{mashiach|anointed ones}}! Do no harm to My prophets!"

units.label: He sent a man before them, Joseph

verses.text: He called down famine on the land and cut off all their supplies of food.
He sent a man before them, Joseph, sold as a slave.
They bruised his feet with shackles and placed his neck in irons,
until his prediction came true and the word of the LORD proved him right.
The king sent and released him; the ruler of peoples set him free.
He made him master of his household, ruler over all his substance,
to instruct his princes as he pleased and teach his elders wisdom.
Then Israel entered Egypt; Jacob dwelt in the land of Ham.
And the LORD made His people very fruitful, more numerous than their foes,
whose hearts He turned to hate His people, to conspire against His servants.
He sent Moses His servant, and Aaron, whom He had chosen.
They performed His miraculous signs among them, and wonders in the land of Ham.
He sent darkness, and it became dark, yet they defied His words.
He turned their waters to blood and caused their fish to die.
Their land teemed with frogs, even in their royal chambers.
He spoke, and insects swarmed, gnats throughout their country.
He gave them hail for rain, with lightning throughout their land.
He struck their vines and fig trees and shattered the trees of their country.
He spoke, and the locusts came, young locusts without number.
They devoured every plant in their land and consumed the produce of their soil.
Then He struck all the firstborn in their land, the firstfruits of all their vigor.

units.label: He brought Israel out with silver and gold

verses.text: He brought Israel out with silver and gold, and none among His tribes stumbled.
Egypt was glad when they departed, for the dread of Israel had fallen on them.
He spread a cloud as a covering and a fire to light up the night.
They asked, and He brought quail and satisfied them with the bread of heaven.
He opened a rock, and water gushed out; it flowed like a river in the desert.

units.label: That they might keep His statutes
units.note: the land given so that Israel might obey

verses.text: For He remembered His holy promise to Abraham His servant.
He brought forth His people with rejoicing, His chosen with shouts of joy.
He gave them the lands of the nations, that they might inherit the fruit of others' labor,
that they might keep His statutes and obey His laws. Hallelujah!

niflaot.gloss: Wonders, marvels, things beyond ordinary explanation. The psalm calls Israel to actively remember, not merely recall, these acts, keeping God's past faithfulness alive as present fuel for worship.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a binding agreement sworn by God Himself. The psalm anchors Israel's whole history, from Abraham to the conquest of Canaan, in this one unbreakable promise, kept for a thousand generations.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one. Here it names the patriarchs themselves, set apart and protected by God among the nations; the same word later narrows to the one Anointed One, the Messiah, in whom God's protection of His people culminates.

basic.context: This psalm retells Israel's story from Abraham to the conquest of Canaan as one continuous act of God's covenant faithfulness. Its opening verses appear almost word for word in David's song when the ark was brought to Jerusalem, 1 Chronicles 16:8-22, showing it was treasured as a summary of Israel's identity: a people who exist because God remembered His promise.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm intentionally tells only one side of the story, God's faithfulness, and says almost nothing of Israel's failures; its companion, Psalm 106, supplies that other side, confessing the people's repeated rebellion. Read together, the pair displays grace to the utterly undeserving: God kept His word not because Israel earned it, but because He had sworn it.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel is called to actively remember, zakar, niflaot, God's wonders, verse 5, keeping the past alive as present worship. Everything rests on His berit, covenant, sworn to Abraham and confirmed to Isaac and Jacob, verses 8 to 10. Even the vulnerable patriarchs are called God's mashiach, His anointed ones, verse 15, under His direct protection.
basic.typology: Joseph, sold as a slave yet exalted to save the very people who wronged him, prefigures Christ, rejected by His own yet exalted to save them. The patriarchs called God's anointed ones, verse 15, anticipate the one true Anointed One, the Messiah, in whom God's protective covenant love finds its fullest expression.
basic.christ: Joseph's path from the pit and prison to the palace, where he saves the very family that betrayed him, is one of Scripture's clearest patterns of Christ: rejected, humbled, and exalted in order to save those who wronged Him, Acts 7:9-10, Genesis 50:20. Luke 1:72-73 shows Mary's song and Zechariah's prophecy both grounding Christ's coming in this same remembered covenant with Abraham.
basic.god: God is shown as utterly faithful to His own word across centuries and generations, protecting a handful of wandering strangers, turning betrayal into rescue through Joseph, humbling the greatest empire of the age through Moses and Aaron, and providing for His people's every need in a barren wilderness, all because He remembered His covenant.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Chronicles 16:8-22 preserves David's earlier version of this psalm's opening verses. Genesis 50:20 states Joseph's own theology of his suffering, you meant evil, but God meant it for good. Acts 7:9-17 retells this same history in Stephen's speech. Luke 1:72-73 grounds the incarnation in God's remembered oath to Abraham.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's rehearsal of covenant faithfulness, kept across centuries of famine, slavery, and wilderness, is the story that Christ Himself fulfills and completes, the promised seed of Abraham in whom all the families of the earth are blessed, Galatians 3:16, Luke 1:72-73.

context.literary: A historical hymn moving chronologically through Genesis and Exodus, opening with a call to praise, verses 1 to 6, then tracing the covenant, verses 7 to 11, the patriarchs, verses 12 to 15, Joseph, verses 16 to 22, Israel's growth in Egypt, verses 23 to 25, the plagues, verses 26 to 36, the exodus and wilderness, verses 37 to 41, and closing with the land given for the purpose of obedience, verses 42 to 45.
context.historical: Its first fifteen verses appear in David's song at the bringing of the ark to Jerusalem, 1 Chronicles 16:8-22, suggesting a Davidic origin later adapted for ongoing temple worship, reminding every generation of Israel that its very existence traces back to God's kept promise.
context.canonical: Forms a deliberate pair with Psalm 106: this psalm rehearses God's faithfulness to the covenant, while Psalm 106 confesses Israel's repeated unfaithfulness in response, together giving a complete and honest account of the relationship between God and His people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To root Israel's identity and worship in the historical, verifiable faithfulness of God across generations, from Abraham's small and vulnerable family to the nation possessing the promised land, so that remembering these acts becomes the engine of ongoing praise and obedience.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific sweep of redemptive history; it prescribes as an abiding pattern the discipline of remembering and retelling God's past faithfulness as the basis for present trust and future obedience, verse 45.

debates.question: Why does this psalm omit any mention of Israel's rebellion, when the history it retells includes so much unbelief?

views.label: The psalm is deliberately one-sided, celebrating grace rather than cataloging failure
views.case: Its purpose is doxology, not comprehensive history; it exists to fuel praise for God's faithfulness, and its companion psalm, 106, was composed to supply the confession of sin this one intentionally omits.

views.label: Read alone, the psalm risks flattering Israel's history
views.case: Without its companion, a reader might conclude Israel's story was one of consistent faithfulness, missing how much of the history it retells, the wilderness wandering, the plagues, in fact accompanied real unbelief and grumbling.

debates.bearing: The two psalms function as a matched pair, placed side by side in the Psalter, one celebrating God's faithfulness and one confessing Israel's unfaithfulness, together displaying grace to a people who did not deserve it, which is itself the shape of the gospel.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm calls Israel to zakar, actively remember, God's niflaot, wonders, verse 5, keeping His past faithfulness alive as present worship. Every act described flows from the berit, covenant, sworn to Abraham and confirmed to Isaac and Jacob, verses 8 to 10, under which even the vulnerable patriarchs are named God's mashiach, His anointed ones, verse 15.

typology.type: Joseph sold as a slave yet exalted to rule and save, verses 17-22
typology.fulfillment: Prefigures Christ, rejected and humbled by His own yet exalted to save those who rejected Him, compare Acts 7:9-10 and Genesis 50:20.

typology.type: The patriarchs named God's anointed ones, mashiach, verse 15
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the one true Anointed One, the Messiah, in whom God's protective covenant love toward His people culminates.

study.christ: Joseph's path from the pit and prison to the palace, where he saves the very family that betrayed him, is one of Scripture's clearest patterns of Christ: rejected, humbled, and exalted in order to save those who wronged Him, Acts 7:9-10, Genesis 50:20. Luke 1:72-73 shows both Mary's song and Zechariah's prophecy grounding Christ's coming directly in this same covenant, remembered and kept, that this psalm celebrates.
study.god: God is utterly faithful to His own word across centuries, protecting a handful of wandering strangers, turning betrayal into rescue through Joseph, humbling the greatest empire of the age through Moses and Aaron, and providing water, bread, and light for His people in a barren wilderness, all because, as the psalm insists twice, He remembered His covenant, verses 8 and 42.

crossReferences.note: Preserves David's earlier version of this psalm's opening verses, sung at the bringing of the ark to Jerusalem.
States Joseph's own theology of his suffering, you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good.
Stephen's speech retells this same sweep of history, from Joseph's betrayal to the exodus, before the Sanhedrin.
Grounds the incarnation itself in God's remembered oath sworn to Abraham.

study.oneStory: This psalm's rehearsal of covenant faithfulness, kept across centuries of famine, slavery, and wilderness wandering, is the very story that Christ fulfills and completes, the promised seed of Abraham in whom all the families of the earth are blessed, Galatians 3:16, Luke 1:72-73.

quiz.q: What does the psalm call Israel to do in verse 5?

options.text: Remember the wonders He has done, His marvels, and the judgments He has pronounced.
options.note: Active remembrance of God's past faithfulness is the engine of the psalm's praise.

options.text: Forget the past and focus only on the present.
options.note: The psalm's entire structure is built on remembering, not forgetting, God's past acts.

options.text: Build a monument to Abraham in Canaan.
options.note: No monument is mentioned; the call is to remember, not to construct a physical memorial.

options.text: Consult the prophets for a new word from God.
options.note: Prophets are mentioned in verse 15 as protected by God, not as the object of the call in verse 5, which is to remember His past wonders.

quiz.q: What did God do for the patriarchs while they were few in number and strangers in the land, verses 12-15?

options.text: He let no man oppress them and rebuked kings on their behalf.
options.note: God's protection over the vulnerable patriarchs, seen in Genesis 12, 20, and 26, shows His covenant faithfulness even before Israel existed as a nation.

options.text: He commanded them to build fortified cities immediately.
options.note: The text shows God Himself protecting them, not a command to build defenses.

options.text: He allowed foreign kings to enslave them for a time.
options.note: The text says the opposite, God rebuked kings and let no man oppress them.

options.text: He sent angels to fight physical battles for them.
options.note: The text specifically names God's rebuke of kings, not angelic warfare, as the means of protection here.

quiz.q: How did Joseph end up ruling in Egypt, according to verses 17-22?

options.text: He was sold as a slave, imprisoned, and then released and exalted by the king.
options.note: The psalm compresses Joseph's whole account, sold, imprisoned, and finally exalted to master his household and instruct princes.

options.text: He was born into Pharaoh's royal family.
options.note: The text explicitly says he was sold as a slave, not born into Egyptian royalty.

options.text: He led a military conquest of Egypt.
options.note: Joseph's rise came through interpretation of dreams and administrative wisdom, not military conquest.

options.text: He purchased his freedom with his own wealth.
options.note: The text says the king sent and released him; it was the king's action, not Joseph purchasing his own freedom.

quiz.q: What was the stated purpose of God giving Israel the lands of the nations, verses 44-45?

options.text: That they might keep His statutes and obey His laws.
options.note: The gift of the land was never an end in itself; it was given so Israel would live in grateful obedience to God.

options.text: That they might become wealthier than all other nations.
options.note: The stated purpose is obedience to God's statutes, not accumulation of wealth for its own sake.

options.text: That they might build a great army to conquer further territory.
options.note: The psalm names obedience to God's law as the purpose, not further military conquest.

options.text: That they might forget their history in Egypt.
options.note: The whole psalm is built around remembering, not forgetting, God's past dealings with them.

quiz.q: How does Joseph's story in this psalm point toward Christ?

options.text: Joseph was rejected and humbled by his own family yet exalted to save them, prefiguring Christ's rejection, humbling, and exaltation to save those who wronged Him.
options.note: Compare Acts 7:9-10 and Genesis 50:20; the pattern of betrayal turned to salvation runs from Joseph to Christ.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: Scripture itself draws this connection, notably in Stephen's speech in Acts 7 and in the pattern of rejection followed by exaltation.

options.text: It predicts the exact geography of Christ's ministry.
options.note: The connection is thematic, rejection turned to salvation, not a matter of predicted geography.

options.text: It shows that suffering always leads to earthly wealth.
options.note: The point is not a formula for material prosperity but the pattern of God turning betrayal into rescue for others.

questions: The psalm calls Israel to actively remember God's wonders, verse 5, not merely to know facts about them. What has God done in your own life that you need to deliberately remember and retell this week?

God protected the patriarchs when they were few, vulnerable, and strangers in the land, verses 12-15. Where do you feel small or exposed right now, and how does God's care for Abraham's family speak to that?

Joseph's suffering, verses 17-19, came before his exaltation, and his own verdict on it was, God meant it for good, Genesis 50:20. What painful chapter of your story might God be using in ways you cannot yet see?

The psalm insists twice that God acted because He remembered His covenant, verses 8 and 42, not because Israel earned it. Where are you tempted to think God's favor toward you depends on your performance rather than His promise?

This psalm is deliberately paired with Psalm 106's confession of failure. How does holding both truths together, God's faithfulness and our unfaithfulness, shape a more honest and grateful kind of praise?

The land was given so that Israel might keep God's statutes, verse 45, not as an end in itself. What blessings has God given you that you have treated as ends in themselves rather than as calls to grateful obedience?

translationNotes.note: Supplies of food renders a Hebrew word literally meaning staff, a picture of the support that sustains life, here cut off by the famine that sends Joseph's family toward Egypt.
To instruct in verse 22 follows the Septuagint and Syriac; the Hebrew Masoretic Text reads to bind, likely reflecting the same underlying idea of Joseph exercising authority over Pharaoh's officials.

Yet they defied His words follows the Septuagint and Syriac; the Hebrew reads more literally, for had they not defied His words, a difficult clause either way pointing to Egypt's continued resistance under the plagues.

meta.notes: BSB text obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 105, since the local scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error. Footnote markers [a] through [d] were stripped; no em or en dashes appear in the source verses for this psalm. Verse count (45) and first/last verse wording cross-checked against PSALMS-SECTION-MAP.md bounds, Psalm 105:1-105:45. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) directly and substantively expounding Psalm 105 could be confirmed this session; only a short Piper article on Psalm 105:4 was found, not a full message, so sermon status is recorded as none rather than fabricated.
